Recovery Services vaults concentrated in eastus (68.9%) while virtual machines are distributed with peaks in westus2 (23.9%), centralindia (20.8%) and southeastasia (18%): because primary compute is not colocated with most recovery vaults, plan to deploy ASR vaults or additional vault capacity nearer the VMware primary sites or set up replication targets that minimize cross-region latency and meet data residency requirements.

50% of Recovery Services vaults use user-assigned identities (UAMI): because the tenant already applies UAMIs to vaults, design ASR orchestration and failover runbooks to authenticate with user-assigned managed identities and grant them least-privilege RBAC on vaults, storage and monitoring resources to maintain a consistent identity model and simplify audits.

Virtual machines overwhelmingly have both system- and user-assigned managed identities (98.8%): because VMs already carry managed identities, plan to use those identities for runbook-driven failover steps (for example, to quiesce workloads, update DNS or call recovery APIs) so you avoid storing long-lived credentials and preserve traceable principal usage in audit logs.

Automation accounts are few (3) with Basic SKU and public network access enabled, and there are 10 runbooks concentrated in eastus and westus2: because the existing automation footprint is small and public-facing, scale Automation accounts and distribute runbook hosts across primary and recovery regions (or use Hybrid Runbook Workers on-prem/VCF) and enable managed identities with network restrictions to ensure high-availability and secure execution during failover.

Operational Insights (Log Analytics) workspaces are widely deployed (149) with public ingestion/query access enabled (~98%): because centralized logging exists and is reachable, integrate ASR diagnostics, runbook execution logs and Recovery Services events into the tenant's Log Analytics workspaces and implement retention/alerting policies there to produce compliant, queryable audit trails.

Key Vaults show strong data-protection posture (100% enablepurgeprotection, 94.4% soft-delete 90 days): because secrets and keys retention/purge protection is standard across the tenant, store DR-related secrets, runbook webhook secrets and any encryption keys in Key Vaults that follow the existing purge/retention policies to meet compliance and forensic requirements.

Recovery Services vaults report infrastructure encryption enabled (100%): because vault-level infrastructure encryption is in use, ensure any cryptographic material used for ASR (snapshots, encryption keys) and related key management steps align with the vaults' encryption posture and documented compliance controls when designing recovery playbooks.

The tenant has heavy use of ExpressRoute cross-connections (170) and a small number of virtual network gateways: because robust private connectivity is already available, plan VMware-to-Azure replication and test failover traffic to traverse existing ExpressRoute circuits or dedicated VPN/peering paths to ensure predictable bandwidth and to satisfy network security/compliance constraints.

Storage redundancy across tenant storage accounts favors Standard_LRS (48.5%) with meaningful usage of GRS/RAGRS (23.3%/17.2%): because storage replication preferences are mixed, choose storage redundancy and snapshot placement for ASR replication artifacts that align with the tenant's resiliency and compliance posture (for example local vs geo-redundant storage) and document cost/availability tradeoffs in the DR plan.

Monitoring and alerting conventions use scheduled query rules and user-assigned identities (75% of scheduled query rules use UAMI) and many Activity Log alerts exist: because monitoring is implemented with identity-backed automation, implement runbook-triggered alerts and scheduled queries for ASR health and failover actions using user-assigned identities so alerting actions are auditable and consistent with existing governance.
